memories.content: Buchholz is a FIDE tiebreaker (Section 8) that sums the scores of all opponents a player faced in a Swiss tournament
Buchholz tiebreaker sums all opponents' scores to break ties in Swiss chess tournaments
Sonneborn-Berger is a FIDE tiebreaker that weights opponents' scores by the result achieved against them
Average Opponents Buchholz is a FIDE tiebreaker (Section 8.2) that calculates the average of opponents' scores
VCL19 is the FIDE checklist used for verifying Swiss pairing software endorsement compliance
VCL19 is the FIDE verification checklist for Swiss pairing engine endorsement — 19 items covering pairing rules, mode requirements, and output format
C.04.3 is the FIDE Dutch System pairing rules section — VCL19 items 1-18 map to specific C.04.3 articles
ScoringSystem type defines how points are awarded for wins, draws, and losses in a chess tournament
FIDE C.07 regulations (effective March 2026) define the official tiebreak systems for chess tournaments
FIDE handbook keeps pairing rules (C.04) and tiebreak rules (C.07) as completely separate chapters — Swiss pairing algorithms never reference any tiebreaker
Chess tournaments have different bye types: full bye (1 point), half bye (0.5 points), pairing bye (given when odd number of players), zero bye (0 points)
Chess tournaments have different bye types: full bye (1 point), half bye (0.5 points), pairing bye, and zero bye (0 points). Each has specific FIDE regulations.
FIDE Section 16 defines 5 categories of unplayed rounds for tiebreak adjustment: pairing-allocated byes, full-point byes, half-point byes, zero-point byes, and forfeit losses
Byes don't affect color selection in Swiss pairings — color history is tracked only from OTB games
@echecs/buchholz package covers FIDE Section 8 tiebreakers: buchholz, averageOpponentsBuchholz, and foreBuchholz
@echecs/tournament is a TypeScript package for managing chess tournaments — version 3.0.0 introduced a full type redesign

The @echecs ecosystem is the program under FIDE endorsement: @echecs/swiss (pairing engine), @echecs/tournament (tournament management), @echecs/trf (TRF16 I/O), and @echecs/endorsement (FPC + RTG tooling)
bbpPairings is a reference FIDE-endorsed pairing engine used to validate tournament pairing software
bbpPairings is the reference FIDE-endorsed pairing engine — the @echecs endorsement compares pairings against it
FIDE (Federation Internationale des Echecs) is the governing body for international chess that provides endorsement for chess pairing software
Roberto Ricca is the SPP department head at FIDE, email r.ricca-tec@rrweb.org — author of JaVaFo (endorsed pairing engine)
@echecs/swiss is the Dutch pairing engine package in the echecs monorepo
Each tiebreaker in the echecs monorepo is its own independent package — one package per tiebreak, not grouped
All tiebreak packages use an identical function signature: (player: string, games: Game[], players: Player[]) => number
All tiebreak packages in the echecs monorepo use an identical Tiebreak function signature for consistency
@echecs/buchholz was the first package implemented in the tiebreak extraction — used as template for the remaining 7
Game type uses black/white instead of blackId/whiteId — consistent naming across all packages
standings() should be in @echecs/tournament, not in pairing packages — standings is a tiebreak consumer, not a pairing concern
FIDE endorsement application must reach the SPPC secretariat at least 4 months before Congress (C.04.A A.7)
For FIDE endorsement, the software must demonstrate correctness by comparing its output against reference implementations like bbpPairings
Every @echecs package must contain a SPEC.md file with the relevant FIDE specification
Each tiebreak package follows identical file layout: src/index.ts (barrel), src/types.ts, src/utilities.ts, __tests__/index.spec.ts
All @echecs packages use ESM-only with NodeNext module resolution, sideEffects: false, vitest for testing
All @echecs packages throw RangeError for domain errors, never generic Error
